stories.headline: Snowstorm drops up to 10 inches across Denver metro, potential record-breaking heat wave to follow by week's end
stories.summary:
A cold front swept through Colorado overnight Saturday, dropping between 0.9 inches at Denver International Airport and 10 inches near Larkspur, with most of the metro recording 2 to 3.5 inches. The Palmer Divide bore the brunt of the storm, accumulating 4 to 10 inches of snow, while mountain areas experienced intense squalls with whiteout conditions. Strong north winds gusting 50 to 55 mph persisted through Sunday morning along the I-70 corridor.

Despite the wintry conditions, National Weather Service forecaster Russell Danielson noted this winter has been far below normal, with DIA recording only 22.8 inches of snowfall since November compared to a seasonal average of 40.5 inches through March 15. The storm brought a brief reprieve but is not expected to close the snowfall deficit.

The snow marks a dramatic prelude to what forecasters are calling a potentially historic heat wave. Temperatures are expected to climb into the low 70s by Tuesday, reach 80 degrees by Wednesday, and surge into the mid-to-upper 80s by Friday and Saturday. The all-time March record of 84 degrees, set on March 26, 1971, could be broken as early as Friday.

stories.headline: Hundreds of flights delayed, 95 canceled at DIA following overnight snowstorm
stories.summary:
An overnight snowstorm disrupted operations at Denver International Airport on Sunday, with 642 flights delayed and 95 canceled as of late afternoon, according to flight tracking service FlightAware. Southwest Airlines canceled the most flights at 40, followed by SkyWest with 29 and Delta with eight. United, American, and Frontier also reported significant delays.

Despite less than an inch of snow accumulating at DIA itself, the broader storm system caused cascading disruptions across multiple carriers. More than half of the delayed flights were departures, while most cancellations affected arriving aircraft.

Travelers are advised to check with their airlines before heading to the airport. Conditions are expected to improve through Monday as the storm system moves east.

stories.headline: Snowstorm causes crashes, road closures across Colorado on Sunday morning
stories.summary:
The overnight snowstorm that blanketed much of Colorado led to slick road conditions Sunday morning, prompting multiple crashes and road closures across the state. A High Wind Watch was in effect for the Front Range and Eastern Plains until 3 p.m. Sunday, compounding hazardous driving conditions. The 9NEWS Weather Impact Team issued a Weather Impact Alert for widespread strong winds, snow squalls, and cold temperatures.

Colorado Department of Transportation reported closures on several roadways as first responders worked to clear crash scenes and treat the affected roads. Motorists were urged to slow down and allow extra travel time throughout the day.

Conditions gradually improved through Sunday afternoon as snow tapered off and winds began to ease across lower elevations.

stories.headline: Challenger Melat Kiros trounces Rep. Diana DeGette in Denver delegate vote, setting up competitive primary
stories.summary:
Melat Kiros, a 28-year-old first-time candidate and former corporate attorney, received nearly twice as many votes as 15-term Democratic incumbent Rep. Diana DeGette at a Denver county party assembly Saturday. Kiros captured 63 percent of the delegate preference poll vote to DeGette's 35 percent, with 2 percent uncommitted. Both candidates are expected to qualify for the June 30 Democratic primary in Colorado's 1st Congressional District.

Kiros, the daughter of Ethiopian immigrants who launched her campaign last July, framed the result as a rebuke of DeGette's three-decade tenure. She cited rising rents, healthcare costs, and the incumbent's corporate PAC fundraising as central issues driving her campaign. The 1st Congressional District covers Denver and portions of Arapahoe County and is the state's most reliably Democratic seat.

DeGette's campaign acknowledged the result but pushed back on the process, citing technical problems with the state party's software platform and questions about ballot eligibility. A third Democrat, University of Colorado Regent Wanda James, is pursuing a primary ballot spot via petition, facing a Wednesday deadline to submit 1,500 valid signatures. The congressional district assembly, where candidates must clear 30 percent delegate support to advance, is scheduled for March 27.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature at midpoint with budget cuts, immigration restrictions and AI regulations still ahead
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers reached the 60-day midpoint of the 2026 legislative session Saturday, with Gov. Jared Polis having signed two dozen bills into law while hundreds more remain in progress. The most consequential work of the session lies ahead, with top budget writers still working to identify roughly $1 billion in program cuts to address a revenue shortfall and rising costs. The legislature must adjourn by May 13.

Among bills already advancing through the Capitol is the HOME Act, which makes it easier for nonprofits and school districts to build housing on land they own. The measure passed the Senate on Thursday and is headed to Polis' desk. Other passed measures include an expansion of who can file for extreme risk protection orders, a ban on 3D-printed guns, and a bill allowing Colorado to seek broader vaccine guidance from medical professional organizations.

Major fights still to come include two prominent immigration restriction bills backed by Democrats, debates over artificial intelligence regulation and data center policy, firearm regulations, and tax policy. Democrats also plan to advance a union-friendly labor bill that Polis vetoed last year. House Majority Leader Monica Duran said the chamber is on track and prepared to work seven days a week if needed to meet the deadline.

stories.headline: Denver man sentenced to 75 years in prison for Facebook Marketplace armed robbery in Aurora
stories.summary:
A Denver man was sentenced last week to 75 years in prison after being convicted of robbing an Aurora woman who had listed jewelry on Facebook Marketplace, according to Arapahoe County court records. Frayer Contreras-Gafaro, 24, was convicted in December on 11 felony charges including second-degree kidnapping, aggravated robbery, and burglary. He will not be eligible for parole until age 84.

Conteras-Gafaro and an unidentified accomplice posed as buyers and pulled out guns inside the victim's apartment in the 1300 block of North Laredo Street on June 12, 2024. The victim's two children were present and were ordered into a nearby room while the men stole approximately $20,000 worth of jewelry. The children were told their mother would be killed if they screamed.

The victim tracked Contreras-Gafaro down using her husband's Facebook account after he blocked her. Assistant Chief Deputy District Attorney Johnny Lombardi said the sentence ensures the defendant cannot "terrorize another family" and called the crime "calculated and violent." A co-defendant has not been publicly identified.

stories.headline: 61-year-old arrested in connection with double shooting on East Hampden Avenue in Denver
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested a 61-year-old individual Sunday in connection with a double shooting that occurred Saturday night on East Hampden Avenue, according to the Denver Police Department. Two people were shot in the incident, though their conditions were not immediately disclosed by authorities.

Few additional details were released by police as of Sunday. The arrest follows a weekend of elevated police activity across the metro area.

stories.headline: Nuggets lose 127-125 in overtime to Lakers after Austin Reaves executes rare intentional-miss free throw play
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets lost 127-125 in overtime to the Los Angeles Lakers on Saturday night after Austin Reaves executed one of the most unusual game-tying plays of the NBA season. Protecting a 118-115 lead with 5.2 seconds left, Denver intentionally fouled Reaves, expecting him to take two free throws — not enough to tie the game. Reaves made the first, then intentionally bounced the second off the front of the rim, chased down his own rebound, and converted a baseline floater with 1.9 seconds left to force overtime.

Lakers coach JJ Redick said he relayed instructions for Reaves to miss toward the single side of the lane, a play he credited entirely to Reaves for executing correctly despite Redick initially calling the wrong direction. Nuggets coach David Adelman called it "one in 100 in the NBA." Nikola Jokic and Aaron Gordon acknowledged they failed to box out Reaves urgently enough on the free throw.

The loss continues what has been a difficult late-game stretch for Denver and allows Los Angeles to take the season series. The Nuggets have now dropped multiple close games this season in the final seconds.

stories.headline: Colorado women's basketball earns No. 10 seed in NCAA Tournament, will face Illinois in Nashville
stories.summary:
The Colorado Buffaloes women's basketball team earned a No. 10 seed in the 2026 NCAA Tournament on Sunday and will face No. 7 Illinois in first-round play in Nashville on Saturday, March 21. The Buffs finished the regular season 22-11 overall and 9-9 in the Big 12, falling to West Virginia in the conference tournament semifinals.

Head coach JR Payne has guided Colorado to four NCAA Tournament appearances, including back-to-back Sweet 16 runs in 2023 and 2024. This marks the program's 17th overall NCAA appearance; the Buffs are 21-16 all-time in tournament play with three Elite Eight appearances.

Illinois enters the matchup at 21-11 after a 64-58 quarterfinal loss to Iowa in the Big Ten tournament. The winners of the two first-round Nashville games — the other featuring host Vanderbilt and High Point — will meet in the second round on Monday, March 23.

stories.headline: Avalanche fall 3-1 in Winnipeg as Jets' Hellebuyck stops 28 shots; Brent Burns sets ironman record
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche dropped a 3-1 decision to the Winnipeg Jets at Canada Life Centre on Saturday, with goaltender Connor Hellebuyck stopping 28 of 29 shots to keep Colorado largely at bay. Winnipeg scored twice in the second period on goals by Kyle Connor and Alex Iafallo before Cole Perfetti added an insurance marker. Martin Necas got Colorado on the board late in the third, with Nathan MacKinnon and Cale Makar each picking up assists.

Defenseman Brent Burns reached a significant milestone in the loss, appearing in his 990th consecutive NHL game and surpassing Keith Yandle for the second-longest ironman streak in league history. Mackenzie Blackwood made 15 saves on 17 shots in goal for Colorado.

The defeat follows Colorado's 5-1 win over Seattle earlier in the week and keeps the Avalanche in a tight Western Conference playoff race. MacKinnon's assist extended his points total as he continues to chase Edmonton's Connor McDavid for the Art Ross Trophy scoring lead.

stories.headline: Metro districts in Colorado: How developer-controlled quasi-governments raise property taxes for thousands of homeowners
stories.summary:
Colorado is home to more than 2,400 metro districts, quasi-governmental entities created under the Special District Act of 1981 that have become a primary mechanism for funding new residential development infrastructure across the state. Created by developers with local government consent, the districts issue bonds to pay for roads, utilities, and other infrastructure, with repayment tied to future property taxes assessed on homes built within the district. Critics note that by the time homeowners join a district's board of directors, the financial arrangements — often negotiated when the board consisted solely of the developer — have already been locked in for decades.

The structure has drawn scrutiny at the state Capitol, where some lawmakers argue the developer-controlled boards create inherent conflicts of interest. Developers frequently purchase the bonds themselves, and because repayment is deferred, compounding interest can dramatically inflate the total cost borne by homeowners. State Sen. Mike Weissman of Aurora has pushed for greater oversight legislation.

The issue has tangible impact for Denver metro homeowners living within districts, who often face significantly higher property tax bills than neighbors in comparable homes outside district boundaries. Developers defend the model, arguing that "development should pay for itself" and that no comparable financing mechanism exists to build out new neighborhood infrastructure.

stories.headline: Missing 14-year-old Jefferson County girl found safe after overnight search
stories.summary:
A 14-year-old girl reported missing from Jefferson County was found safe Sunday afternoon, according to the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office. The girl had last been seen Friday in the 6500 block of South Balsam Court in Littleton, prompting a Colorado Bureau of Investigation missing person alert.

The sheriff's office confirmed her safe recovery in an update posted at 2:58 p.m. Sunday. No additional details about the circumstances of her disappearance or recovery were immediately released.

joke.joke: My wife asked me if I wanted to go to a party. I said, 'Let me check my calendar.' I don't have a calendar. I just wanted to sit there for a second.

topStory.headline: Allies Respond With Caution After Trump Calls for Naval Coalition to Secure Strait of Hormuz
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump called on multiple nations Saturday to dispatch warships to the Strait of Hormuz, the critical Persian Gulf waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes, after Iran moved to restrict passage through the strait in retaliation for U.S. and Israeli military strikes. In posts on Truth Social, Trump named China, France, Japan, South Korea, the United Kingdom and NATO member states as countries he hoped would contribute naval forces, promising U.S. coordination and threatening that nations failing to assist would face a "very bad future." He also warned that the U.S. would continue striking Iranian shoreline positions and targeting Iranian vessels in the meantime.

The nations Trump addressed have responded with notable caution and, in several cases, have declined to make firm commitments. Japan's foreign ministry said it would not immediately dispatch naval vessels, stating that independent judgment is fundamental to its decision-making. France indicated its ships would remain in a defensive posture in the eastern Mediterranean, while South Korea's presidential office said it would carefully review the request. China's Embassy in Washington called for an immediate cessation of hostilities without directly addressing Trump's proposal, though U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Sunday he expects China to be a "constructive partner" in reopening the strait.

Iran has been striking tankers and other vessels attempting to transit the strait since the conflict began approximately two weeks ago, with at least 10 oil tankers reported hit, targeted or attacked according to data from the UK Maritime Trade Operations, the International Maritime Organization and Iraqi and Iranian authorities. Tehran has said the waterway remains open to all countries except the United States and its allies. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said other countries had approached Iran seeking safe passage for their vessels, with India confirming it had negotiated the passage of two Indian-flagged gas tankers on Saturday through diplomatic engagement with Tehran.

Oil prices have climbed above $100 a barrel since the conflict began, with an Iranian official warning prices could exceed $200 a barrel if the situation persists. Approximately 1,000 oil tankers are currently unable to transit the strait. Iran's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, has vowed to keep the waterway closed, while the commander of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy disputed U.S. claims that Iran's military capacity had been destroyed, stating the strait is "under control" rather than militarily blocked.

The calls for a multilateral naval coalition come as the Trump administration faces domestic criticism over what Democratic Senator Chris Murphy described as a lack of a clear plan for reopening the strait. Trump has claimed that 100 percent of Iran's military capability has been destroyed but acknowledged Tehran retains the capacity to deploy drones, mines and short-range missiles in the waterway. Analysts expressed skepticism that China, which continues to receive Iranian oil unimpeded, would commit naval forces alongside the U.S., and no participating nation has announced concrete plans to deploy ships to the region.
